examples: NORTH SIDE STATION -> North Side Station
FIRST AVENUE STATION -> First Avenue Station
fixes.label: Recased shouting stop names

findings.effort: A column in stops.txt; your scheduling software likely has it.
findings.fix: Set wheelchair_boarding to 1 (accessible) or 2 (not accessible) for every stop. A field survey can start with the busiest stops.
findings.what: 6389 of 6389 stops don't say whether a wheelchair user can board there.
findings.why: Riders who use wheelchairs can't plan a trip when accessibility is marked 'unknown'; apps show no information at all.

findings.effort: Often one default setting in your export.
findings.fix: Set wheelchair_accessible on every trip (most small-agency fleets are 100% accessible, so this is often a single default).
findings.what: 17870 of 17870 trips don't say whether the vehicle is wheelchair accessible.
findings.why: Even with accessible stops, riders need to know the bus itself can take them.

findings.effort: Often a bulk fix in your scheduling software.
findings.fix: Rename stops to mixed case (e.g. 'Main St & 2nd Ave').
findings.what: About 6367 stop names are written in ALL CAPS.
findings.why: Mixed-case names are easier to read in apps and are read more naturally by screen readers.

findings.effort: One field.
findings.fix: Add feed_contact_email to feed_info.txt.
findings.what: feed_info.txt has no technical contact (feed_contact_email or feed_contact_url).
findings.why: App makers and state data programs have nobody to email when they spot a problem with your feed, so problems linger.

completeness.name: completeness
completeness.summary: 0% of stops state wheelchair accessibility (0% marked accessible, 0% marked not accessible). This measures what the feed publishes, not whether a stop is physically usable. Fare data is published.

findings.effort: Often a bulk fix in your scheduling software.
findings.fix: Use mixed case for stop names and headsigns (e.g. 'Main St & 2nd Ave', not 'MAIN ST & 2ND AVE').
findings.what: Some rider-facing names are in ALL CAPS or all lowercase.
findings.why: ALL-CAPS stop and headsign names are harder to read in apps and are read awkwardly by screen readers.

findings.effort: A few minutes per flagged trip.
findings.fix: Check the flagged stop times for transposed minutes.
findings.what: Some scheduled trips travel implausibly fast between stops.
findings.why: Usually a typo'd stop time; riders get impossible arrival estimates.

findings.effort: Varies.
findings.fix: Review the rule documentation for 'fast_travel_between_far_stops' at https://gtfs-validator.mobilitydata.org/rules.html and check the flagged rows in your feed.
findings.what: Fast travel between far stops (flagged by the MobilityData validator).
findings.why: See the linked rule for what this affects.

findings.effort: A few minutes per flagged stop.
findings.fix: Check the flagged stops' coordinates and the route shape in your scheduling software; re-snap whichever is misplaced.
findings.what: Some stops sit far from the route line they belong to.
findings.why: Trip planners may draw the bus route through the wrong streets or point riders to the wrong corner.

findings.effort: One setting.
findings.fix: Enable 'remove unused shapes' (or similar) in your export tool.
findings.what: The feed contains route shapes no trip uses.
findings.why: Harmless to riders, but it bloats the feed and suggests stale export data.

findings.effort: A review pass in your scheduling software.
findings.fix: Remove retired stops from the export, or add them back to the trips that should serve them.
findings.what: Some stops exist in the feed but no trip ever stops at them.
findings.why: Riders may walk to a stop where no bus is scheduled to arrive.

findings.effort: Varies.
findings.fix: Review the rule documentation for 'leading_or_trailing_whitespaces' at https://gtfs-validator.mobilitydata.org/rules.html and check the flagged rows in your feed.
findings.what: Leading or trailing whitespaces (flagged by the MobilityData validator).
findings.why: See the linked rule for what this affects.

findings.effort: One field per route.
findings.fix: Pick a darker/lighter route_text_color for the flagged routes.
findings.what: Some route colors don't contrast with their text color.
findings.why: Route badges become unreadable, especially for riders with low vision.

findings.effort: A field at a time; not urgent.
findings.fix: Review the flagged fields and fill in the ones your riders would use.
findings.what: Some files are missing recommended (not required) fields.
findings.why: Recommended fields like agency_phone or stop descriptions make the feed more useful to riders and trip planners.

findings.effort: Varies.
findings.fix: Review the rule documentation for 'missing_feed_contact_email_and_url' at https://gtfs-validator.mobilitydata.org/rules.html and check the flagged rows in your feed.
findings.what: Missing feed contact email and url (flagged by the MobilityData validator).
findings.why: See the linked rule for what this affects.

findings.effort: One setting in most export tools.
findings.fix: Trim past service periods the next time you export.
findings.what: Many of the feed's trips don't run at all in the next 7 days.
findings.why: It usually means old service periods are still in the export, making the feed bigger and harder to check.

findings.effort: Varies.
findings.fix: Review the rule documentation for 'future_calendar' at https://gtfs-validator.mobilitydata.org/rules.html and check the flagged rows in your feed.
findings.what: Future calendar (flagged by the MobilityData validator).
findings.why: See the linked rule for what this affects.

findings.effort: Varies.
findings.fix: Review the rule documentation for 'future_feed' at https://gtfs-validator.mobilitydata.org/rules.html and check the flagged rows in your feed.
findings.what: Future feed (flagged by the MobilityData validator).
findings.why: See the linked rule for what this affects.

correctness.name: correctness
correctness.summary: The MobilityData validator flagged 13 kinds of issue across 16840 instances (0 error, 16838 warning, 2 informational).

freshness.name: freshness
freshness.summary: Service data covers the next 118 days.

findings.effort: A vendor support ticket with the trip ids attached.
findings.fix: Ask your AVL vendor to check vehicle-to-trip assignments for the flagged trips.
findings.what: 41 of 46 sampled vehicle positions were far from their assigned route (worst: 18209 m).
findings.why: A bus shown off its route usually means a wrong trip assignment; riders watch their bus drive the wrong streets.

findings.effort: A vendor data-mapping question.
findings.fix: Spot-check the flagged predictions against what buses actually did; raise trip-matching with your AVL vendor.
findings.what: Some live predictions disagree with the schedule by more than 30 minutes.
findings.why: Differences that large usually mean predictions are keyed to the wrong trips, not that buses are that late.

realtime.name: realtime
realtime.summary: Sampled 9 times outside service hours: 3 of 3 feeds healthy; 10.9% of vehicles on their route; predictions ran a median of 4381s ahead of schedule.

criteria.detail: Service data covers the next 119 days.
States wheelchair access on 0% of stops and 0% of trips; the mark needs 90% of each.

conformance.summary: This feed is close to the conformance mark. States wheelchair access on 0% of stops and 0% of trips; the mark needs 90% of each.

ntd_id_alignment.detail: If you report to the National Transit Database, your GTFS agency_id should be your five-digit NTD ID by report year 2026 (2025 for full reporters). We don't have your NTD ID on file, so this one is not checked yet.

pillars.detail: Service data covers the next 119 days.

ntd_readiness.summary: This feed looks ready for your NTD certification: published at a public URL, valid, and current. The official check is still your D-10 certification.

recommendations.effort: A per-route color setting in your scheduling software.
recommendations.fix: Adjust route_color or route_text_color so each pair clears 4.5:1. Often switching the text between black and white is enough.
recommendations.what: 23 route badge(s) pair a color and text color below the WCAG 4.5:1 contrast bar (1, 12, 26, 28X, 29, and more).
recommendations.why: Low-contrast route badges are hard to read for riders with low vision and wash out on a phone screen in sunlight at the stop.

recommendations.effort: A short text field per affected stop; start with the busiest.
recommendations.fix: Add tts_stop_name with the spoken form, e.g. 'Main Street and Second Avenue', for the affected stops.
recommendations.what: 6257 stop name(s) use abbreviations or symbols a screen reader may mispronounce, with no spoken form set ("BROADWAY AVE AT BELASCO", "BROADWAY AVE AT BELASCO", "BROADWAY AVE AT HAMPSHIRE", and more).
recommendations.why: Riders who use a screen reader hear the raw name, so 'Main St & 2nd Ave' can come out as 'Main saint' or drop the ampersand.

findings.effort: A cleanup pass in your scheduling software.
findings.fix: Remove stops no route serves, or add the trips that should call at them.
findings.what: 28 of 6389 boardable stops are never served by any trip.
findings.why: Riders see these stops in apps and on the map but can never catch anything there, which erodes trust in the data.

top_fixes.effort: A column in stops.txt; your scheduling software likely has it.
top_fixes.fix: Set wheelchair_boarding to 1 (accessible) or 2 (not accessible) for every stop. A field survey can start with the busiest stops.
top_fixes.what: 6389 of 6389 stops don't say whether a wheelchair user can board there.
top_fixes.why: Riders who use wheelchairs can't plan a trip when accessibility is marked 'unknown'; apps show no information at all.

top_fixes.what: 17870 of 17870 trips don't say whether the vehicle is wheelchair accessible.
top_fixes.why: Even with accessible stops, riders need to know the bus itself can take them.

top_fixes.fix: Ask your AVL vendor to check vehicle-to-trip assignments for the flagged trips.
top_fixes.what: 41 of 46 sampled vehicle positions were far from their assigned route (worst: 18209 m).
top_fixes.why: A bus shown off its route usually means a wrong trip assignment; riders watch their bus drive the wrong streets.
